title: Loss, gradients and optimization

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Loss, gradients and optimization; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Artificial Intelligence; August 2023; Machine Learning and Neural Networks: units, optimization, generalization, and architecture families; atomic topic: Loss, gradients and optimization
sources.claim: Neural training evaluates a loss on examples, computes gradients of that loss with respect to parameters, and applies an optimizer update whose scale depends on learning rate, features, batches, and objective.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Neural training evaluates a loss on examples, computes gradients of that loss with respect to parameters, and applies an optimizer update whose scale depends on learning rate, features, batches, and objective.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: For one scalar linear prediction, the learner computes squared loss, its gradient, and one parameter update, then verifies the new loss numerically. Your goal is to trace a simple parameter update and diagnose learning-rate or scale problems. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Supervised learning; Data and experimental workflow; Units, layers and activations; Bias, variance and regularization. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: Neural training evaluates a loss on examples, computes gradients of that loss with respect to parameters, and applies an optimizer update whose scale depends on learning rate, features, batches, and objective. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: A gradient is local, lower training loss need not improve validation, learning rate can diverge or stall, stochastic batches add variance, nonconvex optimization lacks a global guarantee, and labels/objectives encode choices. The learner must reject this overclaim: Gradient descent always moves directly to the global best model and any positive learning rate eventually converges.

includes: Explain how examples, loss and gradient-based updates change parameters without requiring full calculus derivations.
Trace a simple parameter update and diagnose learning-rate or scale problems.
excludes: A gradient is local, lower training loss need not improve validation, learning rate can diverge or stall, stochastic batches add variance, nonconvex optimization lacks a global guarantee, and labels/objectives encode choices.

outcomes: Trace a simple parameter update and diagnose learning-rate or scale problems.

examples.title: Loss, gradients and optimization: bounded direct case
examples.situation: For one scalar linear prediction, the learner computes squared loss, its gradient, and one parameter update, then verifies the new loss numerically.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: For one scalar linear prediction, the learner computes squared loss, its gradient, and one parameter update, then verifies the new loss numerically.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Treat gradients as local loss sensitivity and validate update scale, data, objective, and generalization; convergence and global optimality are not automatic.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Treat gradients as local loss sensitivity and validate update scale, data, objective, and generalization; convergence and global optimality are not automatic.

examples.title: Loss, gradients and optimization: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: A learning rate ten times larger overshoots and increases loss, showing that gradient direction alone does not choose a safe step size.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: A learning rate ten times larger overshoots and increases loss, showing that gradient direction alone does not choose a safe step size.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: A gradient is local, lower training loss need not improve validation, learning rate can diverge or stall, stochastic batches add variance, nonconvex optimization lacks a global guarantee, and labels/objectives encode choices.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: Gradient descent always moves directly to the global best model and any positive learning rate eventually converges.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Gradient descent always moves directly to the global best model and any positive learning rate eventually converges.
misconceptions.correction: Treat gradients as local loss sensitivity and validate update scale, data, objective, and generalization; convergence and global optimality are not automatic.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: Gradient descent always moves directly to the global best model and any positive learning rate eventually converges.

limitations: a toy circuit does not explain large-network representations, training data, emergent behavior, or deployment safety
